items.title: Density Formula
items.subject: Physical Science

studentAnswer.summary: Density tells how much mass is packed into a certain volume.
studentAnswer.approvedText: Density is mass divided by volume. Use D = m / V when you know mass and volume.
studentAnswer.example: If an object has a mass of 20 g and a volume of 5 cm^3, its density is 4 g/cm^3.

items.teacherNotes: Keep formula behavior in lib/formulas. This item is teacher-approved content and metadata only.

items.title: Dmitri Mendeleev
items.subject: Physical Science

studentAnswer.summary: Dmitri Mendeleev was a scientist who organized elements in a way that helped predict missing elements.
studentAnswer.approvedText: Dmitri Mendeleev is known for creating an early periodic table. He arranged elements by patterns in their properties and left spaces where he predicted undiscovered elements would belong.

items.teacherNotes: Good bridge between periodic table structure and evidence-based prediction.

image.altText: Optional teacher-approved image of Dmitri Mendeleev.

items.title: Sputnik 1 Launch
items.subject: Physical Science

studentAnswer.summary: Sputnik 1 was the first artificial satellite launched into orbit.
studentAnswer.approvedText: Sputnik 1 was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957. It was the first artificial satellite to orbit Earth and became a major event in the Space Race.

items.teacherNotes: Useful for connecting physics, engineering, and Cold War science history.

items.title: Measurement Lab Reminder
items.subject: Physical Science

studentAnswer.approvedText: When you record mass, volume, or density, write both the number and the unit. A measurement without a unit is incomplete.

items.teacherNotes: Example of a classroom-specific note that a teacher could approve through a future form.

items.title: Inertia
items.subject: Physical Science

studentAnswer.summary: Inertia is an object's tendency to keep doing what it is already doing.
studentAnswer.approvedText: Inertia means an object resists changes in motion. An object at rest tends to stay at rest, and a moving object tends to keep moving unless a force changes its motion.
studentAnswer.example: A book on a desk stays still until something pushes or pulls it.

items.teacherNotes: Can be linked later to Newton's First Law answers.
